answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: This is the classic starting point of Dynamic Programming. The key lesson is that plain recursion repeats the same computations again and again. DP fixes that by storing results.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Base cases are F(0)=0 and F(1)=1. Then build upward.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Think of the answer for step i. You can only come there from one step before or two steps before. That is why the recurrence is dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: This is structurally similar to Fibonacci.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Now the state represents minimum cost instead of number of ways. The last move can still come from one step before or two steps before, but now you take the cheaper path.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Transition: dp[i] = min(dp[i-1] + cost[i-1], dp[i-2] + cost[i-2])

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: This is a choice DP problem. At each house, either skip it or take it. If you take it, you must skip the previous house. Compare both choices and store the best.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Define dp[i] as the best increasing subsequence ending at index i. Then check all previous indices j. If nums[j] < nums[i], then nums[i] can extend that subsequence.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Initialize all dp values to 1 because every element can form a subsequence by itself.
